segments.s505-g73: ز (poetic form of از) marks origin; here it appears twice to mark two groups: ز دیوارگر ... ز آهنگران = 'from the masons, from the smiths'. هم after دیوارگر functions as 'also/too', linking the two source groups. Word order is emphatic/poetic: prepositional phrases precede the following clause.
segments.s505-g74: هرانکس که ... بود is a relative clause: 'whoever that was ...'. استاد is a noun 'master/expert'; بود is the past-tense copula 'was'. اندرَان (archaic locative) means 'among them' and comes at the end for emphasis: 'whoever was a master among them.'
segments.s505-g75: ز گیتی = 'from the world' (i.e., from all lands). به پیش سکندر شدند literally 'they became to the presence of Alexander' — here شدند (past of شدن) is used idiomatically for 'they came/appeared before' (they presented themselves to Alexander). به پیش = 'into the presence of.'
segments.s505-g76: بدان کار = 'to/for that work' (بدان = به آن in older/poetic spelling). بایسته یاور شد(ند) = 'became (or were) fitting/necessary helpers'; بایسته is adjectival 'fitting/necessary', یاور is 'helper/assistant', and شد(ند) is past 'became/were.'
segments.s505-g77: ز هر کشوری = 'from every country/land'. دانشی here functions attributively ('of knowledge' / 'learned'), and شد (past of شدن) means 'arose/was formed'. گروه is the noun 'group/band'; the verb follows in the poetic order: 'a group of learned men arose from every land.'
segments.s505-g78: دو دیوار کرد = 'made two walls' (دو + object + کرد 'did/made'). از دو پهلوی کوه = 'from the two flanks/sides of the mountain' indicating location/material/position; پهلوی = 'side/flank'. The verb کرد is the simple past 'made.'
segments.s505-g79: ز بن تا سر = 'from the base up to the head/summit' (بن 'base; bottom'); تیغ بالای اوی = 'the ridge/crest above it' (تیغ here = 'ridge/point'); اوی is an archaic possessive 'its'. The prepositional range ز ... تا gives the span from bottom to top.
segments.s505-g80: چو introduces a simile 'as/like'. صد شاه‌رش here reads as 'a hundred (units) of شاه‌رش' (شاه‌رش is a rare/poetic term — treated as a noun); کرده is the past participle 'made' (used here to form the image 'as if a hundred X had made its breadth'), and پهنای اوی = 'its breadth/width' (پهنای = 'the width of', اوی = 'its').
segments.s505-g33: بهاران is the head noun 'spring' (season). It is immediately followed by a relative clause introduced by the old-fashioned relative کز ('which/that'), with ابر 'cloud' as its source and اندر آید 'comes in' (اندر + آید = 'comes into/comes in'). خروش 'roar' resumes the idea: the sequence is noun → relative clause → resumptive noun, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s505-g34: همان here points back to the just-mentioned scene ('that same / that very'); سبز دریا is a metaphorical noun phrase 'green sea' (سبز used in the verdant sense modifying دریا understood as a vast expanse). برآید is a present/habitual verb 'rises/appears', and به جوش is a prepositional phrase 'into/with boil(s)' — literally 'arises to a boil' = 'seethes'.
segments.s505-g35: چو here is the temporal 'when/as'. تنین is the agentive noun 'thunderous sound' (not a monster). ازان موج 'from that wave' (ازان = 'from that/thereof') marks origin, and بردارد is a present tense/transitive verb 'lifts/takes up' with ابر 'cloud' as its object: 'when the thunder lifts a wave from it, (it) lifts the cloud' — compact poetic word order puts the verb before its object.
segments.s505-g36: هوا here means 'the air / atmosphere' (i.e. the wind/air). برخروشد is a verbal form meaning 'is stirred up / rises' (a classical compound/periphrasis for agitation/occurrence). بسان introduces a simile 'like/as', and هژبر is used adjectivally/epithetically ('Hojabr' as a strong/noble epithet) — so 'the air is stirred up like Hojabr'.
segments.s505-g37: فرود افگند is a compound verbal expression in past tense: افگند 'threw/cast' with فرود ('downward') — 'hurled down' or 'let fall'. The direct object follows: ابر تنین 'the thunder-cloud' (ابر modified by تنین). چو کوه is a simile 'like a mountain', so the whole clause reads 'he hurled down the thunder-cloud like a mountain' (typical inverted epic order).
segments.s505-g38: بیایند is a present/plural verb 'they come' (here narratively 'come'), زیشان 'from them' (zī-šan = from/of them) gives source, and گروها گروه is a tautological poetic plural 'groups, company after company / ranks upon ranks' (two synonyms stacked for emphasis).
segments.s505-g39: خورش آن بود = 'that was their food' (خورش here used generally as 'food'). سال تا سالشان is an idiomatic temporal phrase meaning 'year after year' (تا linking the successive years), so the whole segment states a continuing condition: 'that was their food, from year to year.'
segments.s505-g40: که introduces the subordinate result/clause 'that / so that'. آگنده 'laden/filled' is a participial/adjectival form, and گردد (classical present/subjunctive 'becomes / may become') serves as the resultive verb: 'so that (they) become filled'. بر و یالشان 'on body and their mane' (بر = on/upon, و = and, یالشان = their manes) specifies what becomes filled — literal word order places the locatives after the verb phrase, as is common in classical verse.
segments.s505-g49: بسی آفرین یابد از هرکسی is a nominal + verb clause in which آفرین (a noun: 'praise/acclamation') is the internal object and یابد is third-person singular present (classical Persian) meaning 'receives/attains'. بسی is an adverbial intensifier modifying آفرین ('much/many'), and از هرکسی is a prepositional phrase 'from everyone' indicating the source of the praise.
segments.s505-g50: ازان پس به گیتی بماند بسی is temporal + locative + verb: ازان پس ('after that') sets the time, به گیتی is a locative phrase 'in/within the world', and بماند is the third-person present/subjunctive form meaning 'he remains/lasts'. بسی here intensifies the duration ('for a long time' / 'much'). The verb follows its circumstances, as is typical in classical Persian.
segments.s505-g51: بزرگی کن و رنج ما را بساز contains two imperatives. بزرگی کن is an exhortation literally 'do greatness' = 'show greatness/generosity' (بزرگی = noun, کن = 2nd-person imperative). رنج ما را بساز has رنج ما را as a definite direct object (را marking the object 'our suffering') and بساز is an imperative 'make/repair/relieve' — i.e., 'relieve our suffering.'
segments.s505-g52: هم از پاک یزدان نه‌ای بی‌نیاز is an adverbial + source phrase followed by a predicate. هم 'also' qualifies the clause; از پاک یزدان 'from the Pure God' gives the source/context; نه‌ای is an archaic/poetic negative copular form (functionally 'is not' here) linking to the predicate بی‌نیاز ('without need, independent'). The clause therefore says roughly 'also, (you) are not independent of the Pure God' (i.e. do not consider yourself free of need regarding God).
segments.s505-g53: سکندر بماند اندر ایشان شگفت is a clause with subject first (سکندر) followed by the verb بماند 'remained/stayed'. اندر ایشان 'among them' is the locative phrase, and the postposed adjective/participial شگفت ('astonished' / 'in wonder') describes Alexander's state. Word order here places the predicate and circumstances before the adjectival comment.
segments.s505-g54: غمی گشت و اندیشه‌ها برگرفت has a two-part narrative sequence: غمی گشت 'sorrow became/arose' (گشت = 'became/turned' used as past/present narrative 'fell upon him') and اندیشه‌ها برگرفت 'thoughts took hold' where برگرفت (literally 'took up') means 'were seized/held' or 'he was filled with thoughts'. The conjunction و links the two resultant states.
segments.s505-g55: چنین داد پاسخ که از ماست گنج is a reporting clause: چنین داد پاسخ 'thus he gave an answer' (چنین = 'thus', داد = 'gave', پاسخ = 'answer'). The subordinate clause introduced by که states the content: از ماست گنج literally 'the treasure is from us' where از ما is the source, ست (an older copula form) = 'is', and گنج = 'treasure'.
segments.s505-g56: ز شهر شما یارمندی و رنج is a short verbless clause listing what comes 'from your city': ز شهر شما 'from your city' (ز = poetic 'from'), followed by the two coordinated nouns یارمندی ('friendship/favor/allyship') and رنج ('suffering/hardship'). The verb 'is/are' is omitted (understood) and the nouns are presented as things associated with that city.
segments.s505-g105: برو is an archaic/discourse particle placed before the clause to mark a narrative sequence ('then' / thereupon). مهتران is the subject (plural) 'the elders/eldersly chiefs' followed by the past plural verb خواندند 'they called/sang/cried out'; آفرین is the shouted exclamation (direct quoted word) and functions as the content of خواندند.
segments.s505-g106: که introduces the content clause (what they cried). بی‌تو is a prepositional/privative phrase 'without you'. مبادا is a jussive/optative negative ('may it not be' / 'let there not be') and governs the following coordinated nouns زمان و زمین, which are the patients/terms of the wish ('time and earth'). Word order places the optative before its nominal terms, a poetic inversion.
segments.s505-g107: ز + چیزی is a poetic way to mean 'from/of something' or 'from those (something)'; که begins a relative clause 'that/which'. بود is the past copula 'was', and اندران جایگاه is a locative expression ('in that place/status') where اندر + ان (poetic locative) places the referent inside that place; the whole segment means 'from those that were in that place'.
segments.s505-g108: فراوان is an adverb/quantifier 'in great number' modifying the verb. ببردند is past tense, 3rd person plural (they took/brought). نزدیک شاه is a directional/goal phrase 'near the king' (here 'brought close to the king'). Word order (adverb before verb, goal after verb) is idiomatic and concise in the epic style.
segments.s505-g109: نپذرفت is a past, 3rd‑singular negative verb 'he did not accept / refused'. ازیشان (از + ایشان) means 'from them'. The conjunction و links to the next clause; خود is reflexive/emphatic 'himself', and برگرفت is past 3rd‑singular 'took up / took back / assumed' — together 'he refused it from them and took it himself.' The direct object (what was refused/taken) is omitted, recoverable from context.
segments.s505-g110: جهان is the subject 'the world'. مانده is a perfect/past participial sense 'remained / was left' (result state). زان کار is a causal prepositional phrase 'because of that deed' (زان = 'from/for that'), and اندر شگفت places the world 'in wonder' (اندر + noun, archaic locative). The whole clause states the resulting state: 'the world remained in wonder at that deed.'
segments.s505-g89: به خروار انگشت بر سر زدند: the preposition به plus a measure word (خروار) forms an instrumental/quantifying phrase 'with a heap/multitude of fingers.' بر سر is a locative phrase 'upon the head.' زدند is the 3rd-person plural past tense 'they struck' and connects the instrumental phrase and the locative to the verbal action.
segments.s505-g90: بفرمود تا آتش اندر زدند: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb 'he commanded/ordered.' تا introduces a purposive/result clause 'so that/that,' and آتش اندر زدند is a clause meaning 'they struck/put the fire inside' (i.e. they kindled/drove the fire in); زدند is again 3rd-person plural, marking a different agent than the commander.
segments.s505-g91: دم آورد و آهنگران صدهزار: دم آورد is a verbal phrase 'a breath/blast came forth' (دم as 'blast' or 'bellows-blow' and آورد in past tense). و links the two parts. آهنگران followed by صدهزار is a poetic word order meaning 'blacksmiths, a hundred thousand' (i.e. a hundred thousand blacksmiths).
segments.s505-g92: به فرمان پیروزگر شهریار: به فرمان is an agentive/ablative phrase 'by/at the command of.' پیروزگر شهریار is a noun phrase where پیروزگر ('triumphant' / 'victor') qualifies شهریار ('king'), i.e. 'the triumphant king.'
segments.s505-g93: خروش دمنده برآمد ز کوه: خروش دمنده is a genitive compound 'the roar of the blower' (noun + agent noun). برآمد is the past 'arose/issued forth.' ز کوه is the poetic preposition 'from the mountain,' indicating origin.
segments.s505-g94: ستاره شد از تف آتش ستوه: ستاره شد literally 'became a star/appeared as a star' (here metaphorically 'a spark appeared'). از تف آتش is 'from the spitting/blast of the fire' (تف = a spit or blast), and ستوه is an adjectival predicate 'weary' applied poetically to the fire or to the scene; overall the clause says sparks arose from the fire's explosive breath.
segments.s505-g95: چنین روزگاری برآمد بران: چنین روزگاری is a nominal phrase 'such a time' or 'such days.' برآمد is 'arose/occurred.' بران is an adverbial 'thereupon' / 'upon that,' linking this statement to what has just been described.
segments.s505-g96: دم آتش و رنج آهنگران: دم آتش is 'the blast (breath) of the fire' (a genitive relationship). و links the coordinated nouns, and رنج آهنگران is 'the suffering/toil of the blacksmiths' (رنج as hardship experienced by آهنگران). The whole phrase lists the miseries that constituted that time.
segments.s505-g57: برآرم is a first‑person finite verb (archaic/poetic form) meaning “I will set forth / I utter”; the following من is an emphatic subject pronoun placed after the verb. این راه is the direct object phrase “this way/course,” ایشان (“them”) marks the people to whom the way is presented, and به رای attaches as an adverbial phrase “according to counsel/opinion”. Word order is verb–subject–object–indirect phrase, typical of older Persian poetic diction.
segments.s505-g58: بنیروی is an instrumental prepositional compound meaning “by the power of / by virtue of.” نیکی here is a genitival noun “goodness / goodness’s power.” دهش functions as a nominalized idea of giving (used optatively/associatively in the phrase) — “a giving/grant,” and یک خدای is the agent/appeal “(by) one God / may one God.” The whole phrase supplies a blessing/appeal linked to the preceding clause.
segments.s505-g59: یکایک is an adverb meaning “one by one / each in turn.” بگفتند is the past tense plural “they said.” کای is the vocative particle “O,” here addressing شهریار (“king”); together they form the reported address “they said (to him), ‘O king…’.”
segments.s505-g60: This is an optative/blessing clause. ز تو دور literally “away from you,” بادا is an optative auxiliary meaning “may (it be),” and بد روزگار is the noun phrase “bad times / ill‑fortune.” Word order places the beneficiary (تو) early and finishes with the wished condition (بد روزگار).
segments.s505-g61: ز ما opens the clause “as for us / from us.” هرچ باید is a concessive/relative phrase “whatever is required / whatever must be done.” همه بنده‌ایم is a predicate: بنده‌ایم is the first‑person plural copula + noun “we are servants,” with همه (“all/every one”) modifying it — “we are all servants.”
segments.s505-g62: پرستنده باشیم is a hortative/subjunctive construction: پرستنده “worshipper/devotee” plus باشیم (subjunctive/optative of بودن) = “let us be worshippers.” تا زنده‌ایم is a temporal clause “while/as long as we are alive,” with تا introducing the temporal limit.
segments.s505-g63: بیاریم here functions as a first‑person plural verbum (we will bring / we shall provide) — an offer of action. چندانک modifies the extent “to that extent / insofar as,” خواهی تو چیز = “the thing you want” (خواهی is second‑person present “you want,” followed by the pronoun تو). The whole clause promises to supply whatever the addressee desires. (Note: the lemma senses provided for بیاریم in the token list do not match its verbal use here, so the sense index is unavailable.)
segments.s505-g64: کزین بیش literally “from this more” = “beyond this.” کاری نداریم is an idiom meaning “we have no (further) business/work” — کاری here = “matter/business/task,” and نداریم is the present plural “we do not have.” نیز at the end adds “either/also” or softens the statement as an additional remark.
segments.s505-g41: گیاشان (giyāshān) is the subject 'their plants/vegetation'. بود is the past copula 'was' placed after the subject (typical Persian order). زان سپس is an adverbial phrase 'from that / after that' modifying the predicate, and خوردنی is a predicate adjective/noun meaning 'edible / fit to eat' describing گیاشان.
segments.s505-g42: بیارند here functions as a narrative third-person plural verb meaning 'they bring' (poetic variant of بیاورند); the clause states the action following the previous predicate. هر سو ز آوردنی is an adverbial phrase 'from every side / from every direction of bringing' (هر سو = every direction, ز = from, آوردنی = 'that which is brought' / 'bringing'). Note word order: verb + adverbial phrase is used emphatically in this poetical line.
segments.s505-g43: چو سرما بود is a temporal subordinate 'when the cold was' (چو = 'when'). سخت is an adverbial intensifier 'severely / very', placed before the adjective لاغر 'thin / emaciated'. شوند is third-person plural present/subjunctive 'they become' used here as the outcome of the temporal clause.
segments.s505-g44: به آواز is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with a cry / with a voice'. بر here functions as a small particle 'up/on' used with the following predicate, and سان 'like' introduces the simile. کفتر is the comparison term 'dove / pigeon' and شوند again is 'they become' (3pl). Together: 'with cries they (seemingly) become like doves.'
segments.s505-g45: بهاران ببینی is an impersonal/future-facing expression 'you will see in spring / one sees in spring' (ببینی is 2nd sg. subjunctive/optative used narratively). به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner of / in the behaviour of' (به + noun), and گرگ is the simile 'wolf'. The whole says 'in spring you will see (them) behaving like wolves.'
segments.s505-g46: بغرند is a 3rd-person plural verb 'they roar / bellow'. بر سان is 'upon / like' (بر + سان) introducing another simile, followed by پیل 'elephant' and سترگ 'large-eyed / great-eyed' (adjective after noun in poetic word order). The clause reads 'they bellow like a great-eyed elephant.'
segments.s505-g47: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. پادشا is a vocative 'O king' (addressed directly). چاره‌ای is an indefinite object 'some remedy' (چاره + a suffixed ای for 'a certain / some'), and سازدی is an archaic second-person singular perfect/subjunctive 'you would make / you were to devise' — a polite/hypothetical verb form used in classical Persian.
segments.s505-g48: کزین is a fused preposition + demonstrative 'from this / hence from this'. غم دل ما is a genitive chain 'the sorrow of our heart' (دل ما = our heart). بپردازدی is an optative/conditional 2nd-singular verb meaning here 'you would remove / free (us) from' (classical usage); grammatically it completes the conditional started by اگر and parallels سازدی.
segments.s505-g25: همه تن (literally “whole/body”) is the topical noun phrase “their entire body.” پر از موی is a predicate/adjective phrase “full of hair” (پر here = “full (of)”), with از linking ‘full’ to its content. The clause then repeats the topic with و موی همچو نیل — موی (hair) is modified by the simile همچو نیل (“like indigo/black”); the color follows the noun in poetic word order.
segments.s505-g26: This lists body-parts: بر و سینه و گوشهاشان — the items are coordinated (“back and chest and their ears”). گوشهاشان is گوش + the enclitic ش/شان “their.” چو پیل is a simile applying to the preceding list (“like an elephant”). The copular verb is omitted (typical in Persian); understood meaning is “were like.”
segments.s505-g27: بخسپند is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning “they lie down / they sleep”; it is 3rd‑person plural. یکی گوش بستر کنند is a subsidiary clause: یکی (“one/one of them” here = one ear) + گوش (“ear”) + بستر کردن (“to make a bed/cushion of”), with کنند (3rd‑person plural of کردن) as the finite verb — literally “they make one ear a bed.” The subject is implied (they).
segments.s505-g28: دگر = “the other (one).” بر before a noun phrase here functions as “over/on”; بر تن خویش = “over their body” (خویش = reflexive/own, here formed from خوی + enclitic ش). چادر کردن = “to spread/put a cloak (over),” with کنند again 3rd‑person plural. The clause means they lay the other ear over their bodies like a cloak; the verb is explicit (کنند).
segments.s505-g29: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from'; 'هر ماده‌ای' is an indefinite noun phrase 'every female/mother' (ماده + ـای). 'بچه' here means 'young/offspring' (used collectively), and 'زاید' is the 3rd-person present of زاییدن 'to give birth' (used impersonally: 'are born'/'gives birth to'), so the clause literally reads 'From every mother offspring are born a thousand.' Word order is typical older/poetic Persian: preposition + NP + object + verb + numeral.
segments.s505-g30: 'کم و بیش' is an adverbial idiom 'less and more' = 'more or less.' 'ایشان' is the pronoun 'they,' referring back to the offspring. 'که' here functions as a relative/interrogative introducer 'who' or 'that who,' and 'داند' is the 3rd-person present of دانستن 'to know.' 'شمار' is the object 'number/count.' Together the phrase poses the rhetorical question 'who knows their number?'
segments.s505-g31: 'به گرد آمدن' is a verbal-noun construction meaning 'the coming together' or 'gathering.' 'چون' = 'as/when' introduces the comparison/temporal clause, 'ستوران' is the plural 'herds,' and 'شوند' is the 3rd-person plural present of شدن 'to become/are' — so the clause reads 'when (they) gather together and become like herds.' Note the impersonal/collective subject is carried over from the previous sentence (the offspring).
segments.s505-g32: 'تگ' is an adjective/adverb meaning 'single' or 'singly'; here it modifies the following verb. 'آرند' is the 3rd-person plural present (classical/poetic) of آوردن/آرودن as used here intransitively — 'they come' or 'they arrive' — so 'تگ آرند' = 'they come singly/one by one.' 'و' is the conjunction 'and.' 'بر سان' is a two-part comparative 'بر' + 'سان' meaning 'in the manner of / like,' and 'گوران شوند' = 'they become like graves' (گوران = graves, شوند = 3rd-pl. of شدن). Grammatically this is a coordinate clause: 'they come singly, and (in that way) become like graves.'
segments.s505-g17: ز چیزی که…literally 'from/because of something that…'. ز here is the poetic preposition 'because of'. که introduces a relative clause 'that'. ما را is the clitic object/topic marker after the pronoun ('we' as the experiencer). بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to it/unto that', and تاب نیست is a stative predicate 'there is no endurance' — i.e. 'we have no patience/endurance for it' (تاب = endurance). Word order reflects classical Persian: [preposition + noun] + relative clause + experiencer + prepositional phrase + predicate.
segments.s505-g18: ز یاجوج و ماجوج مان خواب نیست — another 'ز' = 'because of'. The coordinate pair یاجوج و ماجوج names Gog and Magog. مان here is irregular in the provided senses and functions as the first‑person plural possessive/pronominal element ('our' / 'we' in classical usage), so the phrase means 'we have no sleep' (خواب نیست = 'there is no sleep'). The clause therefore states the cause ('because of Gog and Magog') and its effect (lack of sleep).
segments.s505-g19: چو آیند بهری سوی شهر ما = 'when they come toward our city.' چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آیند is 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive 'they come/arrive'. بهری (بهر + ی) with سوی gives the direction 'toward' (بهری سوی = toward). شهر ما = 'our city' (noun + possessive pronoun).
segments.s505-g20: غم و رنج باشد همه بهر ما = 'sorrow and suffering will be (for) all of us.' غم و رنج are the nominal subject(s). باشد here is a literary 3rd‑person copular/predicative form indicating existence or occurrence ('there will be' / 'is to be'); in classical Persian this form often marks what will befall. همه بهر ما 'all for us' = 'for all of us' / 'befall all of us.'
segments.s505-g21: همه رویهاشان چو روی هیون = 'all their faces like the face(s) of beasts.' همه modifies the pluralized face (رویهاشان = روی + plural + possessive 'their'). چو = 'as/like' (simile). هیون is a poetic word for 'beast/monster', so the clause is a straightforward simile comparing their faces to animals'.
segments.s505-g22: زبانها سیه دیده‌ها پر ز خون = 'their tongues black, their eyes full of blood.' This is a paratactic description: زبانها سیه = 'tongues (are) black' (predicate adjective with omitted copula), دیده‌ها پر ز خون = 'the eyes (are) full of blood' where پر ز + noun is the idiom 'full of X' (پر = 'full (of)'; ز = 'of/from' here). Word order is noun + predicate, typical of descriptive lines in poetry.
segments.s505-g23: سیه روی و دندانها چون گراز = 'black‑faced and the teeth like a boar.' سیه modifies روی (face), forming a compound adjective 'black‑faced.' و links the next noun phrase دندانها (their teeth). چون = 'like' introduces the simile comparing the teeth to a grawl (گراز = 'wild boar').
segments.s505-g24: که یارد شدن نزد ایشان فراز — که introduces a clause giving a corollary or result. یارد (companion/helper) with شدن forms a verbal nominal clause 'to become a companion' or 'to be made a helper.' نزد ایشان = 'in their presence' or 'before them.' فراز is a predicative adverb/adjective 'on high/exalted.' Together the clause says that 'becoming a companion (to them) is exalted' or 'companionship before them is elevated' (a terse, poetic statement of status).
segments.s505-g97: گهرها (plural) is the subject 'the jewels'; the phrase یک اندر دگر is an idiomatic, inverted object/phrase meaning 'one into another' (یک = one, اندر = in/within, دگر = another). ساختند is the past tense, 3rd-person plural perfective 'they made/did/assembled' and completes the clause. Word order is subject — (object phrase) — verb, typical of Persian poetic compression.
segments.s505-g98: وزان is the fused poetic form of و + از آن meaning 'and from that' (linking to the previous clause). آتش تیز is a noun followed by its adjective (noun + adjective order common in poetry): 'keen/fierce fire'. بگداختند is the past, 3rd-person plural of گداختن (with the b- perfective prefix) meaning 'they melted' (or 'were melted' depending on transitivity in context).
segments.s505-g99: ز here is the causal/prepositional ز = 'because of' (poetic). The phrase یاجوج و ماجوج is its object ('Gog and Magog'); گیتی is the grammatical subject 'the world'; برست is the past 3rd-person singular 'burst/was broken'. The clause order is causal phrase — subject — verb, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s505-g100: زمین is the subject 'the earth/ground'; گشت is the past of گشتن used copulatively here meaning 'became'. جای introduces the predicate 'a place/spot for ...'; خرام and نشست are coordinated nouns/nominal actions: خرام (here nominalized as 'proud strutting/gait') and نشست ('sitting/settling'). The conjunction و links the two nominal predicates. Overall: 'the earth became a place of strutting and settling.'
segments.s505-g101: برش here functions as a measure/extent/section (an archaic or poetic term for a dimension). پانصد is the numeral 'five hundred'. بود is the past copula 'was'. بالای اوی is a locative phrase 'above it/on top of it', where اوی is an archaic 3rd-person possessive/pronoun 'its'/'of it'. The construction gives a stative measurement: 'its (vertical) measure above it was five hundred.'
segments.s505-g102: چو is used here as a comparative/approximative marker 'as/about' introducing the quantity (چو سیصد = 'about/like three hundred'). The token بدی is irregular in this context (textual/archaic form) and here functions grammatically like a past copula or measure marker (effectively 'was'); its exact classical sense is uncertain in modern lexicons. نیز 'also' links this clause to the previous one; پهنای اوی is 'its breadth' (پهنای = width, اوی = his/its).
segments.s505-g103: ازان is the fused poetic form of از + آن meaning 'from that' or 'from it'. نامور is an adjective 'famed/renowned' modifying سد 'dam/barrier'. اسکندری is a proper adjectival form 'of Alexander/Alexandrian'. The whole is a noun phrase: 'from that famed dam of Alexander.'
segments.s505-g104: جهانی is the subject 'the world/the universe'; برست is the past 3rd-singular of برستن 'burst/was broken/was torn'. از here functions causally 'because of' (the third sense listed) and introduces the reason phrase بد داوری 'ill/bad judgment' where بد is the adjective 'bad' modifying داوری 'judgment'. The clause reads 'the world was shattered because of bad judgment.'
segments.s505-g9: بپرسید is an archaic/past 3rd-person-plural form meaning 'they asked' (classical -ید ending used for past). کایدر is an archaic interrogative 'whence/where from'. چه باشد شگفت is an indirect question: چه (what) + باشد (present/subjunctive 'is/ may be' used in reported speech) + شگفت (noun/adjective 'wonder/marvel'), i.e. 'what sort of wonder it is'.
segments.s505-g10: کزان is a relative 'from which/whence' linking back to 'شگفت'. برتر اندازه is a nominal phrase 'a higher/superior measure' (برتر modifies اندازه). نتوان گرفت is a negative modal construction: نتوان = 'cannot' and گرفت here 'take' (in the sense 'be taken' or 'be applied'), so together 'no higher measure can be taken'.
segments.s505-g11: زبان here means 'speech/utterance' (they 'opened their tongues' = they began to speak). برگشادند is past 3rd-plural 'they opened/spoke'. بر before شهریار means 'before' or 'to' the king (locative/benefactive: addressing the king).
segments.s505-g12: به + a verb-noun: به نالیدن functions as a purposive/participial phrase 'to lament' or 'with lamentation'. از introduces the topic or cause ('concerning/about'). گردش روزگار is a genitive-like compound 'the turning/cycle (گردش) of time/fate (روزگار)' meaning the vicissitudes of fortune.
segments.s505-g13: که begins a subordinate clause meaning 'for/because'. ما را is the dative/experiential construction 'to us' used idiomatically to mean 'we have' (there is for us). یکی کار 'one/certain matter' and پیش است 'is before/lying ahead'; سخت is a postpositive adjective 'difficult'. The phrase literally: 'that for us one matter lies ahead, difficult.'
segments.s505-g14: بگوییم is the 1st-person-plural subjunctive/volitional 'let us say / we should tell' (expressing intention or proposal). با marks the addressee 'to/with', شاه is 'king', and پیروزبخت is an epithet 'fortunate/fortunate-of-fortune' qualifying the king.
segments.s505-g15: بدین is a locative/demonstrative opening the phrase 'in/with this'. کوه سر is an inverted order for 'سرِ کوه' ('the mountain's head/top'); poetic inversion is common. تا به introduces extent 'as far as/up to', and ابر اندرون 'the inside of the cloud(s)' indicates vertical reach (the mountain's summit reaching into the clouds).
segments.s505-g16: دل ما is the possessive construction 'our heart(s)'. پر از is the adjectival phrase 'full of' where از introduces the contents. The nouns following (رنج و دردست و خون) list what the heart is full of: suffering (رنج), pain / immediate distress (دردست here treated as a nominal compound 'at hand / distress'), and خون 'blood'. The conjunction و simply links the items.
segments.s505-g81: 'ازو' is the prepositional clitic 'from it/him' placed up front; 'یک رش انگشت' is a compact measure phrase: 'یک رش' (a small sprinkling) qualified by 'انگشت' (a finger as a unit) = 'a finger's pinch' (poetic measure). 'و' is the simple conjunction 'and'; 'آهن یکی' places the numeral 'یکی' after the noun in poetic word order to mean 'one piece of iron' (a single iron/steel piece). No finite verb appears in this clause; the action is supplied by the surrounding lines.
segments.s505-g82: 'پراگنده' is an adjective 'scattered/widely spread' placed before the noun 'مس' (copper). 'در میان' is an older two-word locative ('in the middle/among'), and 'اندکی' at the end is an adverb/quantifier 'a little'. Poetic word order places the modifier and locative before the final limiting adverb: 'scattered copper in the middle, a little.'
segments.s505-g83: 'همی' is the classical continuous/iterative marker (here giving an ongoing or habitual past sense: 'were pouring' / 'poured repeatedly'); 'ریخت' is the verbal stem meaning 'poured' (here concrete action); 'گوگردش' is 'گوگرد' + possessive clitic '-ش' = 'their/his sulphur'; 'اندر میان' is the older locative 'into/within the midst'. The clause reads literally 'they poured their sulphur into the middle.'
segments.s505-g84: 'چنین' = 'thus/so'; 'باشد' is the classical present/subjunctive/coptula form often used to state a general truth or wish ('is' or 'let it be so'); 'افسون' = 'charm/spell'; 'دانا' = 'wise' (an attributive adjective); 'کیان' here addresses the royal audience ('O kings' or 'Kayan kings') — poetic inversion leaves the predicate ('باشد') before the noun phrase. The line reads as a comment: 'Thus is the charm, O wise kings.'
segments.s505-g85: 'همی ریخت' again marks the continuous/past pouring action ('they poured/kept pouring'); 'هر گوهری' is the distributive 'each jewel'; 'یک رده' is the measure/result 'one rank/one row/one tier' (object of pouring). Poetic order puts the habitual verb before the quantified noun phrase.
segments.s505-g86: 'چو' is the conjunction 'when/as'; 'از ... تا' marks a span or extent ('from ... up to'); here 'از خاک تا تیغ' = 'from the earth up to the blade'; 'شد' is past of 'شدن' = 'became'; 'آژده' is an adjectival state 'terrifying/dreadful' (poetic/archaic). Together: 'when it came from the ground up to the blade, it became terrifying.'
segments.s505-g87: 'بسی' here is an adverb/quantifier 'much/a great deal' placed before the nouns; 'نفت و روغن' are coordinated materials ('naphtha/oil and oil'); 'برآمیختند' is the past plural verb 'they mixed together' (prefix 'بر' + 'آمیختند' = 'mixed/combined'). The clause means 'they mixed a lot of naphtha and oil.'
segments.s505-g88: 'همی' again marks continuous or immediate action; 'بر سر' is the prepositional phrase 'upon the top/of the surface of' (here 'over'); 'گوهران' = 'gems' (direct object); 'ریختند' is the past plural 'they poured'. Poetic inversion places the adverb first, then the prepositional phrase, then the verb: 'they poured (it) upon the gems.'
segments.s505-g65: This clause contains a simple narrative sequence: 'سکندر بیامد' (Alexander came) uses the past-tense verb بیامد. 'نگه کرد' is the compound verb نگه‌کردن (here split into نگه + کرد) meaning 'to look/stop/observe' with کرد as the light verb; کوه (mountain) is placed after the verb phrase as the object/topic. The word order (subject + past verb + light-verb + object) is typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s505-g66: بیاورد is a third-person past/subjunctive/optative form of آوردن used here with a causative/reporting sense: 'had brought' or 'let/caused to bring'. زان (from that/by that) marks the agent/source, and فیلسوفان گروه is the noun phrase 'group of philosophers' (فیلسوفان + گروه). Together it reads 'had the mountain brought by that group of philosophers.'
segments.s505-g67: بفرمود is an honorific past tense meaning 'he commanded/said'. What follows, 'کاهنگران آورید', is reported direct speech with آورید as the 2nd-person-plural imperative 'bring (you, pl.)' and کاهنگران as the direct object (here 'kahan-people' / 'priests' per the provided senses). The clause structure is reported-order: verb of saying + quoted imperative.
segments.s505-g68: This is a noun list joined by و (and) followed by the repeated imperative آورید at the end, so all listed items are the objects of 'bring'. مس and روی are nouns joined by و; پتک گران is a noun (پتک, 'sledge/hammer') followed by the adjective گران ('heavy/weighty') — adjective follows noun in Persian. The final آورید governs the whole list as the imperative 'bring'.
segments.s505-g69: A list of materials joined by و (and): کج (literally 'crooked', here 'crooked beams/irregular timber'), سنگ ('stone'), and هیزم ('firewood'). فزون ('more/abundant') follows the list to qualify them, and از شمار is a prepositional phrase 'beyond counting' (از = 'beyond/of', شمار = 'number/count'). The whole phrase acts as the quantified object of the previously implied imperative.
segments.s505-g70: بیارید is an imperative 'bring' (2nd-person-plural) here repeating/continuing the command; چندانک + آید به کار is a subordinate clause meaning 'to the extent that it will come to use' or idiomatically 'as much as will be needed.' چندانک is a degree word 'so much/that much,' آید ('come') with the phrase به کار ('to use') yields 'be of use/needed.'
segments.s505-g71: بی‌اندازه ('without measure/excessively') is an adverb modifying the past-tense verb بردند ('they took/carried away'). چیزی که خواست is a relative clause 'the thing(s) that was/were wanted/required' with که introducing the clause and خواست as the past-tense 'wanted/was required.' The clause states they took far more than necessary.
segments.s505-g72: چو شد is a temporal clause 'when it was' (چو = 'when', شد here functioning as auxiliary 'was/ became'), followed by ساخته ('made' — past participle) modifying کار و اندیشه ('the work and the thought/plan'). راست ('right/true') is the predicate adjective qualifying both nouns. Together: 'When the work was made, the work and the thought were correct.'
segments.s505-g1: سوی باختر شد = “turned toward the west”: شد (past of شدن) here means ‘became/turned’ and takes the direction phrase 'سوی باختر' as its complement. چو خاور بدید = 'when he saw the East': چو is a temporal subordinating particle 'when', and بدید is simple past 'saw'. The whole clause reads as a temporal clause ('he turned ... when he saw ...').
segments.s505-g2: ز گیتی همی رای رفتن گزید = 'he chose the plan of going away from the world': ز + گیتی = 'from the world'; همی is a poetic particle (here an emphatic/temporal marker: 'thereupon'/'indeed'); رای (plan, counsel) is the noun that takes رفتن (the infinitive 'to go') as its complement; گزید is the past verb 'chose'. The clause therefore expresses a decision ('he chose the counsel of departing').
segments.s505-g3: بره‌بر یکی شارستان دید پاک = 'he saw a certain clean town nearby': the sequence places the adverbial/phrase 'بره‌بر' (here functioning as a spatial modifier 'just ahead / close by' in poetic diction) before the numeral 'یکی' ('a certain') and the noun 'شارستان' ('town'), with دید as the past verb 'saw' and پاک as a postpositive adjective 'clean/pure'.
segments.s505-g4: که نگذشت گویی بروباد و خاک = 'which (town) had not been traversed; it seemed (as if) by wind and dust': که introduces a relative clause modifying 'شارستان'. نگذشت is past negative 'did not pass/was not passed over'. گویی is a parenthetical particle 'one would say / it seemed' (poetic), and بروباد و خاک ('by wind and dust' / 'wind and dust') supplies the things that, it seems, had not passed/affected the town. Overall: 'so untouched that one would say wind and dust had not passed it.'
segments.s505-g5: چو آواز کوس آمد از پشت پیل = 'when the sound/call of the drum came from behind the elephant': چو is the temporal 'when'; آواز کوس is a genitive-like nominal group 'the sound/call of the drum'; آمد is past 'came'; از پشت + پیل is the prepositional phrase 'from behind the elephant'.
segments.s505-g6: پذیره شدندش بزرگان دو میل = 'two great ones received him': پذیره here is the reception/acceptance notion used in a verbalized/nominalized way; شدندش is a compound with the past plural شدَند plus the enclitic object pronoun -ش 'him' (so the whole conveys 'they received him' / 'they gave him reception'); بزرگان ('the great ones') is the plural subject and the following دو میل is an epithet/measure attached to them (poetic phraseology). Word order places the receiving action before the explicit subject for emphasis.
segments.s505-g7: جهانجوی چون دید بنواختشان = 'when the world‑seeker saw them, he struck/played at them': جهانجوی is a descriptive epithet 'world‑seeker / ambitious man'; چون introduces the temporal clause 'when/as'; دید is past 'saw'; بنواختشان is a past verb with the third‑person plural object suffix -شان 'them' and here means 'struck' or 'caused to sound (i.e. struck the instrument or struck at them)'.
segments.s505-g8: به خورشید گردن برافراختشان = 'he lifted their necks toward the sun': به خورشید marks direction ('toward the sun'); گردن (fronted for emphasis) is 'neck'; برافراختشان is the past verb 'raised aloft' with the suffix -شان 'their/them' — together 'raised their necks toward the sun' (a poetic image indicating exaltation/pride or an imposed posture).

sentences.s505-s37: From the masons and from the smiths, whoever among them was a master.
sentences.s505-s38: From across the world they came into Alexander's presence; they became the proper helpers for that task.
sentences.s505-s39: From every land a band of learned men was formed; they made two walls from the two flanks of the mountain.
sentences.s505-s40: From the base up to the summit of its ridge above, its breadth was as if a hundred shah-rash had made it.
sentences.s505-s17: When spring comes in from the clouds, that green sea breaks into a roar and seethes.
sentences.s505-s18: When the thunderous sound lifts a wave from it, the air is stirred up like (the) mighty Hojabr.
sentences.s505-s19: The thunder-cloud hurled itself down like a mountain; from it come ranks upon ranks.
sentences.s505-s20: That was their food year after year, so that their bodies and manes became filled.
sentences.s505-s25: He will receive much praise from everyone; after that he will remain long in the world.
sentences.s505-s26: Show greatness and remedy our suffering; also, (do not) be independent of the Pure God.
sentences.s505-s27: Alexander remained among them astonished; sorrow came upon him and thoughts seized him.
sentences.s505-s28: Thus he answered that the treasure is from us; from your city (are) friendship and hardship.
sentences.s505-s53: Then the elders cried “Afarin!” — “Without you, may time and earth not be.”
sentences.s505-s54: They took many of those who were in that place and brought them near the king.
sentences.s505-s55: He would not accept (it) from them and took it himself; the world was left amazed at that deed.
sentences.s505-s45: They struck his head with a heaping mass of fingers; at his command they drove fire into it.
sentences.s505-s46: A blast was produced, and a hundred thousand blacksmiths (came) at the command of the triumphant king.
sentences.s505-s47: The blower's roar rose from the mountain; sparks appeared from the fire's spitting.
sentences.s505-s48: Thus a time came upon them — the blast of the fire and the toil of the blacksmiths.
sentences.s505-s29: I will put forward this course to them according to counsel; by the power of goodness, may God grant it.
sentences.s505-s30: One by one they said, “O king, may bad fortune be far from you.”
sentences.s505-s31: As for us, whatever is required, we are all servants; let us be worshippers while we live.
sentences.s505-s32: We will bring whatever thing you wish, so much as you desire; beyond this we have no further business.
sentences.s505-s21: Their plants were, from then on, edible; from every side they brought food.
sentences.s505-s22: When the cold was severe they grew very thin; with cries they became like doves.
sentences.s505-s23: You will see spring acting like a wolf; they roar like an elephant with a huge eye.
sentences.s505-s24: If, O king, you devised some remedy, you would rid our hearts of this sorrow.
sentences.s505-s13: Their whole bodies were covered with hair, the hair as black as indigo; their backs, chests, and ears were like an elephant's.
sentences.s505-s14: They lay down: one (ear) they made into a bed, the other they spread over their bodies as a cloak.
sentences.s505-s15: From every female a thousand young are born — more or less; who can count them?
sentences.s505-s16: When they gather together into herds, they come singly, and like graves they are formed.
sentences.s505-s9: Because of something we cannot endure; because of Gog and Magog we have no sleep.
sentences.s505-s10: When they come toward our city, sorrow and suffering will befall all of us.
sentences.s505-s11: All their faces are like the faces of beasts; their tongues black and their eyes full of blood.
sentences.s505-s12: Black‑faced and with teeth like a boar's; to become a companion in their presence is exalted.
sentences.s505-s49: They set jewels one within another, and by that keen fire they melted.
sentences.s505-s50: Because of Gog and Magog the world burst; the earth became a place for proud strutting and settling.
sentences.s505-s51: Its height above it was five hundred; its breadth likewise was three hundred.
sentences.s505-s52: From that famed dam of Alexander the world was shattered, because of ill judgment.
sentences.s505-s5: They asked, "What wonder is this, whence comes it, that no higher measure can be taken of it?"
sentences.s505-s6: They opened their mouths before the king, to lament the revolutions of fate.
sentences.s505-s7: For we have one difficult matter before us; let us tell the fortunate king.
sentences.s505-s8: Within our hearts—up to the summit of that mountain and into the clouds—they are full of suffering, pain, and blood.
sentences.s505-s41: From it they took a finger's pinch and a single piece of iron, and scattered a little copper in the midst.
sentences.s505-s42: They poured their sulphur into the midst; thus is the charm, O wise kings.
sentences.s505-s43: They poured each jewel in a single row; when it rose from the earth to the blade, it became terrifying.
sentences.s505-s44: They mixed a great deal of naphtha and oil, and poured it over the gems.
sentences.s505-s33: Alexander came, paused and looked, and had the mountain brought by that group of philosophers.
sentences.s505-s34: He commanded, 'Bring the priests/kahan-people; bring copper and zinc and heavy sledgehammers.'
sentences.s505-s35: Bring crooked beams, stones, and firewood in abundance beyond counting—bring as much as will be needed.
sentences.s505-s36: They carried off an excessive amount of whatever was wanted; when the work was completed, both the task and the design were proper.
sentences.s505-s1: When he saw the East, he turned westward, and he resolved to leave the world.
sentences.s505-s2: He saw, just ahead, a certain clean town; it seemed as if neither wind nor dust had ever passed over it.
sentences.s505-s3: When the sound of the war-drum came from behind the elephant, two great nobles received him.
sentences.s505-s4: When the world‑seeker saw them, he struck (at) them and raised their necks toward the sun.
